coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly efficient green emissive carbon dots towards UV encryption fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Xiang
Zhang, Yuyu
Chen, Xiao
Zhong, Yuqin
Wu, Shaogui
Hao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Blue and red emitting carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive research interests, while the research on green emissive CDs (G-CDs) are often ignored. In this work, G-CDs with high photonluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were synthesized through a microwave assisted solvothermal method, where 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid (ANSA) and ethylenediamine were used as precursors, and ethanol as solvent. The chemical structure, surface composition and optical properties of G-CDs suggest that the G-CDs have a structure of carbon core coated by functional groups such as hydroxyl, sulfinyl, and amino groups. The mass fractions of C, N, O and S elements are 75.83%, 14.26%, 9.31% and 0.8%, respectively. The excitation and emission wavelengths are 439 nm and 528 nm, respectively. The G-CDs have considerable photonluminescence efficiency with QY up to 33.8%, which exceeds those of many other G-CDs. The G-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior, indicating that they possess a unique PL center. Furthermore, the possible synthesis mechanism and a simple application of the G-CDs are briefly introduced. This work is of great value for filling the gap of G-CDs with high PLQY and promoting the practical application of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green emissive CDs
Microwave assisted solvothermal method
Photonluminescence quantum yield

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis of the G-CDs.
Structural characterization of G-CDs (a) FT-IR spectrum. (b) Wide scan XPS spectrum (C–F) High resolution XPS of C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p respectively.
Optical characteristics of G-CDs. (a) three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum, (b) two-dimensional projection, (c) UV–Vis absorption (purple), excitation (blue), emission spectra (multi-color).
Single emission behavior. (a) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (b) Normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
The effect of solvent on the PL properties of G-CDs. (a) Fluorescence spectra. (b) Fluorescence intensity. (c) Emission wavelength.
(a) Integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance (a). Comparison of PLQY of G-CDs in different solvents (b).
Schematic diagram of the solvothermal synthesis of G-CDs (a) and the possible autocatalytic reaction mechanism (b).

Application of G-CDs as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink. The painting on a Chinese tranditional lantern is invisible under sunlight (a) while is visible under UV light (b). Note: the green leaves are painted by G-CDs and the red flower is drawn with other R-CDs.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly efficient green emissive carbon dots towards UV encryption fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Blue and red emitting carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive research interests, while the research on green emissive CDs (G-CDs) are often ignored. In this work, G-CDs with high photonluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) were synthesized through a microwave assisted solvothermal method, where 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid (ANSA) and ethylenediamine were used as precursors, and ethanol as solvent. The chemical structure, surface composition and optical properties of G-CDs suggest that the G-CDs have a structure of carbon core coated by functional groups such as hydroxyl, sulfinyl, and amino groups. The mass fractions of C, N, O and S elements are 75.83%, 14.26%, 9.31% and 0.8%, respectively. The excitation and emission wavelengths are 439 nm and 528 nm, respectively. The G-CDs have considerable photonluminescence efficiency with QY up to 33.8%, which exceeds those of many other G-CDs. The G-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior, indicating that they possess a unique PL center. Furthermore, the possible synthesis mechanism and a simple application of the G-CDs are briefly introduced. This work is of great value for filling the gap of G-CDs with high PLQY and promoting the practical application of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most popular carbon nanomaterials after fullerenes, carbon nanotubes and graphene [1,2]. Due to their low toxicity, stable photonluminescence (PL) properties, good biocompatibility, simple synthesis and wide source of raw materials, CDs have broad application prospects in many fields, such as medical diagnosis and treatment [3,4], display equipment [5–7], environmental monitoring [8–10], biological imaging [11–14] and so on. Since their discovery in 2004 [15], CDs have attracted considerable research interests. However, most of the early synthesized CDs are featured by short emissive wavelength, while CDs with long emissive wavelengths are rare. Recent years, more and more orange or red fluorescent CDs (O-CDs or R-CDs) with high photonluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) have been synthesized. For example, Sun et al. synthesized O-CDs with emission wavelength of 580 nm and PLQY up to 64% by silicon doping strategy [16]. Yuan et al. synthesized R-CDs by solvothermal method using resorcinol as precursor, which have an emission wavelength of 610 nm and PLQY up to 72% [17]. In addition, the synthesis scale has also been greatly improved. Niu et al. synthesized R-CDs by solid state synthesis, which could produce kg-grade R-CDs and the conversion rate of raw materials was up to 75% [18]. However, the study of CDs with high PLQY in the middle wavelength band (500 nm–600 nm) is easily ignored.

There are many methods for synthesizing CDs, including solvothermal method, arc discharge method, hydrothermal method, microwave-assisted heating, etc. Compared with other methods, solvothermal synthesis of CDs has the advantages of simple equipment, easy control of conditions and easy synthesis of CDs with high PLQY [19]. In this work, using 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid (ANSA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors, and ethanol as solvent, we synthesized G-CDs using a microwave-assisted solvothermal method. The G-CDs have fluorescence emission wavelength at 528 nm, and PLQY is as high as 33.8%, which exceeds those of many G-CDs at present. This work is of great value for synthesizing green luminescent CDs and promoting the application range of CDs.

In this work, the G-CDs were synthesized using a microwave assisted solvothermal method [20,21]. About 20 mg of ANSA and 100 mg of EDA were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol and ultrasonicated for about 10 min. The mixture was transferred to a 30 ml polytetrafluoroethylene microwave digestion tank, followed by heating at 700 w for 30 min, and then cooled naturally. The obtained reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm min−1 for 10 min to remove insoluble impurities. Afterwards, the supernatant was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous acetate fiber filter membrane and then dialyzed with a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight 500) for 48 h to obtain the G-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1 .

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR): G-CDs solution was placed in a vacuum oven and fully dried at 60 °C. The obtained G-CDs powder was mixed with KBr in the ratio of 1:100, grinded, pressed, and then FT-IR measurement was carried out with scanning range 4000 cm−1-400cm−1.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS): The surface composition of dried G-CDs was determined by a Geneis 60s X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with C1s (284.6eV) as the internal standard.

Quinine sulfate was employed as a standard reference to estimate the PLQY of G-CDs. According to the literature [22,23], quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution has a PLQY value of 0.54 at 360 nm. Then the relative PLQY of test sample could be estimated by the following equation,
(1)
PLQY U
=
PLQY S
⋅
F U
F S
⋅
A S
A U
⋅
(
η S
η U
)
2
where the subscripts S and U refer to the standard substance and test sample, respectively; F represents the integral fluorescence intensity; A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength; η denotes the refractive index of solvent. In order to avoid the interference of inner filter effect, the absorbances (A) of both standard substance and test sample should be less than 0.05.

The chemical structure of G-CDs was characterized by FT-IR and XPS. Fig. 2 a displays the FT-IR spectrum. The peak at 3500-3300 cm−1 is O–H/N–H stretching vibration peak [24,25]; The peak at 1656 cm−1is CO/CN stretching vibration peak [26,27]; The peak at 1346 cm−1 is C–N stretching vibration peak [28]. The peak at 1050 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration absorption peak of SO; the peak at 659 cm−1 is the S–C stretching vibration absorption peak [29]. The above results indicate that G-CDs contain groups such as -NH2, -CO and SO, and may also contain functional groups such as -OH, and -COOH. Fig. 2b displays a wide-scan XPS spectrum of G-CDs, which shows four characteristic binding energy peaks, namely, C1s (285.18 eV), N1s (399.2 eV), O1s (532.1 eV) and S2p (168.6eV), indicating that the G-CDs mainly contain the four elements, and their relative content ratio of C/N/O/S is 75.83%, 14.26%, 9.31% and 0.6%. Fig. 2c displays a high resolution XPS spectrum for C1s, which contains three characteristic carbon peaks 284.8eV (C–C/C = C), 285.6eV (C–N), 288.7eV (C = O) [30]. Fig. 2d is N1s spectrum, where two peaks at 399.2eV and 400.6eV are attributed to C–N–C and N–H respectively [31]. Fig. 2e displays the O1s spectrum, where two peaks at 533.8eV and 532.1eV are attributed to S = O and C = O respectively [32,33]. Fig. 2f shows a high-resolution spectrum of S2p, which has an absorption peak at 168.6 eV, corresponding to the SO functional group [34,35].

UV–vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum are the basic means to characterize the optical properties of fluorescent materials. The fluorescent intensity and wavelength provide relevant information about the environment, the aggregation state, and their interactions for fluorescent particles. Fig. 3 a shows a three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum for G-CDs, where three excitation peaks are clearly observed. The two-dimensional projection is displayed in Fig. 3b, where one can see that the excitation region is mainly located at 300–500 nm while the emission region is mainly located at 500–600 nm. Fig. 3c displays the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (blue), emission spectrum (multi-color) for G-CDs. The UV–vis absorption spectrum has a strong absorption band within 300 nm, which is a typical absorption band of aromatic benzene ring, originating from the
π →
π ∗
electronic transition of sp2 hybrid of carbon atoms [36]. Careful observation reveals that there are three distinct absorption peaks at 308 nm, 343 nm and 427 nm, respectively. These absorption peaks are consistent with the excitation peaks, indicating that these absorption peaks are characteristic absorption peaks, where the absorbed photons may participate in the fluorescence excitation and emission activities. These absorption peaks may be attributed to the
n →
π ∗
electronic transitions of the functional groups on the surface of the G-CDs, such as -NH-, -SO, -NH2 or other groups [37]. Therefore, these functional groups are likely to be part of the luminescent center. The excitation and emission spectra show that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are 439 nm and 528 nm. The filling color below the emission spectrum curve is the fluorescence color corresponding to the emission wavelength. One can find that the emission peak is mainly colored with green, confirming that the product CDs are G-CDs. Furthermore, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission spectrum is 77 nm, indicating that G-CDs have good uniformity.

In order to elucidate the PL mechanism of G-CDs, the PL behavior under different excitation wavelengths were investigated. The excitation wavelengths were sequentially changed in the range of 400 nm to 490 nm, and the evolution of the emission spectra were measured, as shown in Fig. 4 a. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity changes at different excitation wavelengths while the emission wavelengths are relatively close. For observation, these emission spectra are normalized as shown in Fig. 4b. One can clearly see that these emission wavelengths almost coincide, roughly around 528 nm. This result suggests that the G-CDs has a single emission behavior independent of excitation wavelength, which is significantly different from those of many CDs reported in the literature [1]. G-CDs have a typical core-shell structure, the interior is a graphene-structured carbon core, coated by many functional groups such as -CO, -SO, -NH2 on the outer surface. The presence of these heteroatom-containing groups destroy the conjugated structure of graphene and create structural defects at the edges of graphene. Since graphene is unable to emit light by itself, the PL behavior of G-CDs is closely related to these localized conjugated systems with surface defects, regardless of the particle size (quantum size effect) of G-CDs [38–40]. This single emission behavior suggests only one defect really contributes to the light-emitting behavior of G-CDs. Therefore, no matter how the excitation wavelength changes, the emission wavelength of the synthesized G-CDs is almost unchanged. From the above analysis, the G-CDs may have a unique luminescent center, which is closely related to these defects.

In order to study the effect of solvent on the PL behavior of G-CDs, water (WAT), methanol (MET), ethanol (ETH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and NN-dimethylformamide (DMF) were chosen as solvents respectively. The fluorescence spectra of G-CDs of the same concentration in these solvents were measured as shown in Fig. 5 a. For comparison, their peak emission intensities and wavelengths are shown in Fig. 5b and c. One can see that G-CDs have the highest fluorescence intensity in acetone and the lowest in water. The general trend is ACE > ETH > THF > DMF > MET > WAT. In addition, the emission wavelengths of G-CDs in different solvents are also slightly different. Among them, G-CDs have the maximum emission wavelength in water while the minimum in acetone. The overall trend is just opposite to that of fluorescence intensity. From the above observations, it can be seen that the solvent has a great influence on the PL properties of G-CDs. G-CDs display the best PL performance only under the condition of certain solvent polarity.

In order to further quantitatively evaluate the effect of solvent, the PLQY of G-CDs were measured in different solvents. Using methanol, ethanol, acetone, tetrahydrofuran, DMF and water as solvents respectively, the fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra of G-CDs of different concentrations in these solvents were acquired, and then their integral fluorescence intensities were plotted against the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, as shown in Fig. 6 a. According to Eq. (1), the fitting line with larger slope represents a higher PLQY value. Taking quinine sulfate as a reference, one can estimate their PLQY, as shown in Fig. 6b. It is clear that G-CDs have different PL ability in different solvents. Their PLQYs are 33.8% (ACE) > 31.4% (DMF) > 30.9 (THF) > 28.3% (ETH) > 21.5% (MET) > 6.32% (WAT). It is clear that G-CDs have the highest PLQY in acetone and lowest PLQY in water. Note that the PLQY value of 33.8% is not a small value, indicating that the currently synthesized G-CDs have considerable PL ability, which has already exceeded those of many other G-CDs.

The PL mechanism of CDs remains unclear. Although many CDs have graphene core structure, graphene itself is unable to emit light, and the contribution of graphene core to PL is difficult to convince. These CDs synthesized from pure carbohydrates are mostly characterized by low emission efficiency and short emission wavelength. N, S doping can significantly enhance the luminous efficiency of CDs and lead to red shift of emission wavelength. Obviously, the PL of CDs is closely related to N, S elements. The introduction of these elements can change the electron distribution of the PL center to increase the fluorescence intensity. In this work, G-CDs were synthesized by ANSA and EDA, as shown in Fig. 7 a. ANSA serves as not only a carbon source, but also a supplier of N and S elements. EDA can also be used as carbon and nitrogen sources. As illustrated by Fig. 7b, ANSA and EDA might undergo dehydration and deamination reaction at high temperature and high pressure to generate small molecule copolymers, which further polymerize to form large conjugated structures. As suggested by the single emission behavior, G-CDs might have a unique PL center, which is independent of the size of G-CDs. It is speculated that the PL center of the G-CDs is closely related to the surface defects caused by SO and -NH2 at the edges of the G-CDs.

Fluorescent CDs have broad application prospects in many fields, such as display devices, energy conversion, chemical sensing and so on. Here serving as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink is taken as an example to briefly introduce their application. The product solution of the G-CDs can be directly used on ordinary pens, seals and inkjet printers as UV-encrypted fluorescent ink after simple filtration treatment without further purification. The written or printed content is invisible or has weak traces under ordinary daylight, but it is clearly visible under an ultraviolet lamp, so as to achieve the purpose of UV anti-counterfeiting or information encryption. If the G-CDs solution is diluted to a certain extent, such as using raw solvent or water, the inner filtration effect caused by the high concentration can be further eliminated to realize a better effect. Fig. 8 displays a Chinese tranditional lantern, where an ink painting was drawn using CDs as fluorescent inks. These leaves were drawn with G-CDs while the flower was drawn with other R-CDs. The pattern is barely visible in daylight, as shown in Fig. 8a. However, when the lantern is exposed to ultraviolet light, a colorful picture, with green leaves and red flower, is clearly distinguishable, as shown in Fig. 8b. In fact, the fluorescence emission of many CDs is responsive to changes in pH. For example, their fluorescence might be quenched in strong acid or strong base environment, but the fluorescence can be recovered after adjusting the pH to near neutral value. This characteristic allows them be used for UV-pH double encryption fluorescent ink.

In summary, the G-CDs were synthesized by microwave assisted solvothermal method using ANSA and EDA as precursors and ethanol as solvent. The chemical structure, surface composition and optical properties of G-CDs were characterized by FR-IR, XPS, fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. The results show that the G-CDs have a structure of carbon core coated by functional groups such as hydroxyl, sulfinyl, and amino groups, and the mass fractions of C, N, O and S elements are 75.83%, 14.26%, 9.31% and 0.8%, respectively. The G-CDs have emission wavelength of 528 nm and PLQY up to 33.8%, which exceeds those of many other G-CDs. Furthermore, the G-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength-independent emission behavior, indicating they have a unique luminescent center. This work is of great value for synthesizing multi-color CDs with high luminescent efficiency and promoting the practical applications of CDs.

Xiang Long: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis, paper writing. Yuyu Zhang: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis. Xiao Chen: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis. Yuqin Zhong: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis. Shaogui Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis. Liang Hao: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
